title: Texas vs Purdue — Sweet 16 Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • Sweet 16 • San Jose, CA
venue: SAP Center, San Jose, California • CBS
extraInfo: Winner advances to the Elite Eight in the West Region

teams.name: Texas
teams.record: 21-14 • First Four → Beat BYU → Beat Gonzaga

teams.name: Purdue
teams.record: 29-8 • Big Ten Champs • Beat Queens • Beat Miami

stakes.callout: The tournament's best Cinderella meets its most decorated offense. Texas has won three games in nine days — including a last-second game-winner, an upset of BYU, and a stunning takedown of No. 3 Gonzaga — to become the first First Four team to reach the Sweet 16 since UCLA's 2021 Final Four run. Purdue carries the No. 2 offense in America, the NCAA's all-time career assists leader, and a coach who just notched his 500th win. Oh, and one of Texas's key players — Camden Heide — transferred from Purdue this offseason. He hit the game-sealing three against Gonzaga. This is a blockbuster.

cards.label: For Texas
cards.value: Sean Miller's first year at Texas was supposed to be a rebuild. Instead, he's 9-1 all-time in games with a Sweet 16 spot on the line — the best winning percentage of any coach with 10+ games since 1975. The Longhorns are the lowest remaining seed in the tournament. Miller insists this isn't a Cinderella story: "I don't think we ever want to sign up to be the Cinderella story, because we are the University of Texas."

cards.label: For Purdue
cards.value: Matt Painter just won his 500th game at Purdue and is chasing the program's first national championship in men's basketball — ever. The Boilermakers have been to the title game twice (1969, 2024) and lost both times. Braden Smith is the NCAA's all-time career assists leader. This senior core of Smith, Kaufman-Renn, and Loyer has played together for four years. This is their last shot.

cards.label: The Heide Connection
cards.value: Camden Heide spent three seasons at Purdue before transferring to Texas. He hit the game-sealing corner three against Gonzaga with 14.7 seconds left — his only points of the game. Now he faces his former teammates with an Elite Eight berth on the line. Painter on Heide: "I like Cam. I was happy for him."

cards.label: The Style Clash
cards.value: Texas has held opponents to 1.03 points per possession across three tournament games — a defensive resurgence nobody saw coming from a team ranked 159th in defensive efficiency before March. Purdue has the No. 2 offense in the country and shoots 37.9% from three. Something has to break.

styles.title: First Four to Sweet 16 — And They're Not Done
bullets: Tournament defense is real: Texas ranked 159th in adjusted defensive efficiency before the tournament. In three NCAA games, they're allowing just 1.03 points per possession. They held NC State to 66, limited BYU to 71 (despite Dybantsa's 35), and contained Gonzaga to 68. The defense that was a liability all season has arrived at the right time.

Ball security is elite: Texas committed just 5 turnovers against Gonzaga and 9 against BYU. Against Purdue's mediocre defense, limiting turnovers isn't about survival — it's about getting high-quality shots every possession. They had 20 assists on 31 baskets vs. Gonzaga.

Vokietaitis is dominating: The 7-foot Lithuanian is averaging 18.3 PPG and 11.0 RPG in the tournament. He had 21/14 against BYU and 17 against Gonzaga. He shoots 62% from the field and punishes teams inside. Purdue allowed opponents to shoot 56%+ from inside the arc during Big Ten play.

Five scoring threats: Swain (17.7 PPG), Vokietaitis (15.5), Mark (13.6), Pope (13.1), and Heide (46% from three off the bench). This isn't a one-man show. Different players have stepped up in each game — Mark in the First Four, Vokietaitis vs. BYU, Pope and Heide vs. Gonzaga.

Weakness: Season-long three-point defense remains a concern. Texas is in the bottom third nationally in three-point defense. Purdue shoots 37.9% from deep with Loyer at 42.3%. If Loyer and Smith start hitting from the perimeter, Texas's defensive resurgence gets its toughest test yet.

styles.title: The No. 2 Offense in America — Peaking at the Right Time
bullets: Elite offensive efficiency: Purdue ranks 2nd in KenPom offensive efficiency (125.5) and shoots 49.9% from the field. Against Queens they shot 63% from the field and 58% from three. Against Miami: 53% from the field and 57% from three. This offense is on fire.

Loyer is scorching: Fletcher Loyer scored 24 points on 6-of-7 shooting against Miami and is shooting 54% from three over his last five games. When Loyer is hitting, Purdue's inside-out attack becomes virtually unguardable — defenses can't pack the paint because he'll burn them from deep.

Smith makes everyone better: Braden Smith's 9.0 APG runs the show. He broke Bobby Hurley's 33-year-old NCAA career assists record. He had 26/8 against Queens and 12/8 against Miami. Even when he struggled (8 turnovers vs. Miami), Purdue won by 10 because the system doesn't depend on one player.

Six-game winning streak: After losing four of their last six regular-season games, Purdue has won six straight — four Big Ten Tournament games (as a 7-seed, first ever to win the BTT) plus Queens and Miami. They're peaking.

Weakness: Defense ranks 188th in KenPom. Smith's career-high-tying 8 turnovers against Miami showed he can be pressured. And the FDU ghost lingers — Purdue lost to a 16-seed in 2023, a 15-seed in 2022, and a 13-seed in 2021. Three straight years of double-digit seed losses. Texas is an 11-seed with momentum.

keys.title: Purdue's three-point shooting vs. Texas's three-point defense.
keys.description: This is the game. Purdue shoots 37.9% from three as a team, with Loyer at 42.3% (and 54% over his last five games). Texas ranks in the bottom third nationally in three-point defense. In the tournament, Texas has defended better — but they haven't faced an offense like this. If Loyer, Smith, and Cox are hitting from deep, Texas has to extend their defense, which opens up Kaufman-Renn and Cluff inside. If Purdue's shooters go cold like they did against Kennesaw State (1-of-9 from three in the first half), Texas can pack the paint and grind.

keys.title: Vokietaitis in the paint vs. Purdue's interior.
keys.description: Vokietaitis has been the tournament's breakout star: 18.3 PPG and 11.0 RPG across three games. He's 7-0, shoots 62% from the field, and Purdue allowed opponents to shoot 56%+ from inside the arc during Big Ten play. But Purdue has size — Kaufman-Renn (6-9, 240) and Cluff (6-11, 250, 69.8% FG) are no pushovers. This is the best interior matchup Vokietaitis has faced. If he can score 18+ and control the glass, Texas can win. If Purdue neutralizes him, Texas becomes a jump-shooting team against the No. 2 offense in America.

keys.title: Can Texas maintain its five-turnover magic?
keys.description: Texas committed just 5 turnovers against Gonzaga — the best defense in the country — and 9 against BYU. That ball security has been the backbone of their tournament run. Purdue's defense ranks 188th, so they're not going to force turnovers through pressure. But Smith is an elite ball hawk (he caused havoc in the Big Ten Tournament). If Texas stays near single digits, they'll get quality shots. If the turnovers creep above 12-13, Purdue's transition offense will punish them.

keys.title: Braden Smith's turnovers — is Miami a blueprint?
keys.description: Miami's athletic guards harassed Smith into a career-high-tying 8 turnovers. Shelton Henderson particularly bothered him. Texas has Chendall Weaver — the team's best perimeter defender, who had 11 points and 10 rebounds in the First Four — and Dailyn Swain, who defends multiple positions. If Texas can replicate Miami's defensive pressure on Smith and force turnovers, the assists record-holder becomes a liability. Purdue won despite 8 Smith turnovers vs. Miami, but Loyer shot 6-of-7. Texas needs to pressure Smith AND contest Loyer's threes.

keys.title: Camden Heide's insider knowledge.
keys.description: Heide spent three years at Purdue and knows Matt Painter's system, tendencies, and personnel. He knows where Loyer likes to catch the ball, how Smith reads the floor, and what Purdue runs out of timeouts. Miller will mine that knowledge. It's a real edge — not a decisive one, but in a Sweet 16 game where the spread is 6.5-7.5 points, any edge matters. And Heide proved against Gonzaga that he'll step up in big moments.

prediction.summary: Texas has defied expectations all tournament, but Purdue represents a different challenge than anything they've faced. The Boilermakers have the No. 2 offense in America, they're shooting 57% from three in the tournament, and Loyer is on a heater (54% from three over his last five games). Texas's tournament defensive resurgence (1.03 PPP allowed) is real, but it's been tested against Gonzaga's sputtering offense, BYU without Saunders, and NC State in freefall. Purdue is none of those things. The key: if Texas can limit turnovers (they've been extraordinary at this), get 18+ from Vokietaitis inside, and somehow contest Loyer's threes, they can keep it close. But Purdue's depth, offensive firepower, and Smith's playmaking should ultimately prove too much. Expect Texas to make a run — maybe two — but Purdue's system grinds teams down over 40 minutes.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Texas comes out with the defensive intensity that's defined their tournament run. Weaver picks up Smith full-court. Vokietaitis establishes position early and scores on two of his first three touches — the 7-footer has been dominant all tournament. But Purdue's offense is a different animal. Loyer hits an early three off a Smith feed, and Kaufman-Renn muscles inside for a bucket. The pace is faster than Texas wants but slower than the Queens blowout. It's 17-15 Purdue, and both teams are feeling each other out.

phases.title: Mid-First Half Through Halftime
phases.description: This is where Purdue's three-point shooting becomes the story. Cox hits a corner three — he's been Purdue's secret weapon all tournament (11 points vs. Miami, all from three). Then Loyer splashes another from the wing. Texas's bottom-third three-point defense is being exposed. Miller calls timeout. Texas responds through Vokietaitis inside and a Swain drive, but Purdue's spacing is pulling the defense apart. Smith has 6 assists at the half but also 3 turnovers — Texas's perimeter pressure is bothering him. Purdue leads 38-33.

phases.title: Second Half — Texas Makes Its Run
phases.description: Miller adjusts at halftime. Texas switches to a zone look that confuses Purdue's motion offense — the same adjustment that worked against Gonzaga. Tramon Mark — the sixth-year veteran who's been in the Final Four, the Sweet 16, and hit the game-winner in the First Four — scores 7 straight. Jordan Pope, who lit up NC State for 28 in Maui and had 17 against Gonzaga, nails a three. The deficit is 2. The SAP Center crowd, mostly neutral, starts pulling for the Cinderella. Purdue calls timeout. Painter steadies his team.

phases.title: Final 8 Minutes
phases.description: Purdue's system reasserts itself. Smith finds Kaufman-Renn for a layup out of the timeout — his interior scoring has been the counter to every opponent's defensive adjustment all year. Loyer hits a pull-up three that pushes the lead to 7. Texas answers through Vokietaitis, but the Lithuanian big man picks up his fourth foul with 5 minutes left. Without their interior anchor, Texas becomes a perimeter team — exactly what Purdue wants. Smith hits clutch free throws down the stretch (Purdue shot 95% from the line vs. Miami). Heide hits a late three to make it interesting, but Purdue's offensive depth and free-throw shooting close the door. The Cinderella run ends.

projectedScore.rationale: Purdue's three-point shooting (37.9% as a team, Loyer on a heater) exploits Texas's biggest weakness. Vokietaitis keeps Texas in it with 18+ inside, and the Longhorns' ball security limits easy transition points. But Purdue's depth, Smith's playmaking (even if imperfect), and Loyer/Kaufman-Renn's scoring prove too much over 40 minutes. Texas makes it competitive through three quarters before Purdue pulls away.

players.name: Matas Vokietaitis
players.stats: 18.3 PPG, 11.0 RPG in NCAA Tournament • 62.0% FG
players.bio: The tournament's breakout star. The 7-footer from Marijampole, Lithuania — whose mother played for Lithuania's national age-group teams — has been dominant: 21 points and 14 rebounds vs. BYU, 17 points vs. Gonzaga. AAC Freshman of the Year at Florida Atlantic before transferring to Texas. Shoots 62% from the field. His interior game is the X-factor — Purdue allowed opponents to shoot 56%+ inside the arc during Big Ten play. If Vokietaitis can bully Kaufman-Renn and Cluff, Texas can win. His free-throw shooting (.691) is the one weakness to exploit.

players.name: Dailyn Swain
players.stats: 17.7 PPG • 7.6 RPG • 3.4 APG • SEC Newcomer of the Year
players.meta: F • 6-7 • Junior • Columbus, OH • Xavier Transfer • Projected 1st-Round Pick
players.bio: Texas's most complete player and a projected first-round NBA pick. Followed Sean Miller from Xavier to Austin. The only player in a major conference to lead his team in points, rebounds, assists, steals, and minutes. Had 13 in the First Four, 14 vs. BYU, and 11 vs. Gonzaga — he hasn't needed to dominate because others have stepped up. But if Texas needs a takeover game, Swain has it in him. #1 nationally in isolation points per possession earlier this season.

players.name: Tramon Mark
players.stats: 13.6 PPG • Game-Winner vs NC State • 6th-Year Senior
players.meta: G • 6-5 • Dickinson, TX • Houston → Arkansas → Texas
players.bio: The ultimate March veteran. From Dickinson, Texas. Has played at Houston (2021 Final Four, 2023 Sweet 16), Arkansas, and now Texas — 139+ career games across six years. Hit the game-winning fadeaway with 1.1 seconds left to beat NC State in the First Four, then scored 15 vs. BYU. His fourth career game-winner under 5 seconds. In a tight game, you want the ball in his hands.

players.name: Camden Heide
players.meta: F • 6-7 • Junior • Wayzata, MN • Former Purdue Player
players.bio: The most intriguing subplot of this game. Heide spent three seasons at Purdue but wanted a bigger role and transferred to Texas. Against Gonzaga, he played just 13 minutes but hit the game-sealing corner three with 14.7 seconds left — his only points of the game. Now he faces his former teammates with an Elite Eight on the line. He knows Purdue's system inside and out. Painter: "Very similar to the shot he made last year against Houston in the corner... I like Cam. I was happy for him."

players.name: Braden Smith
players.stats: 14.3 PPG • 9.0 APG • NCAA All-Time Assists Leader
players.meta: G • 6-3 • Senior • Westfield, IN • Consensus All-American
players.bio: The most prolific passer in college basketball history. Broke Bobby Hurley's 33-year-old NCAA career assists record (1,077+) during the Queens game. Had 26 points (10-15 FG) and 8 assists vs. Queens, then 12 and 8 against Miami — though he was harassed into 8 turnovers (career-high tie) by Miami's athletic guards. Both parents played college basketball at Arkansas Tech. Led Westfield High to its first sectional title in 105 years. The engine of the No. 2 offense in America.

players.name: Fletcher Loyer
players.stats: 24 pts vs Miami (6-7 FG) • 42.3% 3PT • 54% 3PT last 5 games
players.meta: G • 6-4 • Senior • Fort Wayne, IN • Basketball Family
players.bio: On the hottest shooting streak of the tournament. Scored 24 points on 6-of-7 shooting against Miami and is hitting 54% from three over his last five games. His father John is a scout for the LA Clippers and was interim coach of the Detroit Pistons. His grandfather played at Purdue in 1964. Won the national High School 3-Point Championship. Loyer directly attacks Texas's biggest weakness — three-point defense. When he's hitting, Purdue's offense is virtually unguardable.

players.name: Trey Kaufman-Renn
players.stats: 14.0 PPG • 8.5 RPG • 57.9% FG • 19 pts, 9 reb vs Miami
players.meta: F • 6-9, 240 lbs • Senior • Sellersburg, IN • Philosophy Major
players.bio: The interior anchor. Chose Purdue over Indiana, North Carolina, and Virginia. Majoring in Philosophy. Had 25 points and 9 rebounds vs. Queens, then 19 and 9 against Miami — he's peaking at the right time after his scoring dipped from 20.1 PPG last season to 14.0. When Smith feeds him in the post, he finishes at an elite rate (57.9% FG). His matchup with Vokietaitis is the game within the game.

players.name: Oscar Cluff
players.stats: 10.6 PPG • 7.4 RPG • 69.8% FG • No. 1 Transfer in America
players.meta: F • 6-11, 250 lbs • Senior • Sunshine Coast, Australia
players.bio: From Australia. Ranked as the No. 1 transfer in the country when he committed to Purdue from South Dakota State, where he was one of only 10 NCAA players to average 17+ points, 12+ rebounds, 2+ assists while shooting 60%+ — alongside Zach Edey, Blake Griffin, and Tim Duncan. Had 9 points, 11 rebounds, 5 assists, and 4 blocks against Queens. Shoots 69.8% from the field. The second half of Purdue's imposing interior duo.

funFacts.title: The Purdue Transfer (Texas)
funFacts.body: Camden Heide spent three seasons at Purdue before transferring to Texas. He hit the game-sealing corner three against Gonzaga with 14.7 seconds left. Now he faces his former teammates. His father is also a Purdue alum. Painter: "I like Cam. I was happy for him."

funFacts.title: First Four to Sweet 16 (Texas)
funFacts.body: Texas is the first team since UCLA in 2021 to go from the First Four to the Sweet 16. That UCLA team went all the way to the Final Four as an 11-seed. Texas is the lowest remaining seed in the tournament and has won three games in nine days.

funFacts.title: The All-Time Assists King (Purdue)
funFacts.body: Braden Smith broke Bobby Hurley's 33-year-old NCAA career assists record during the Queens game, recording his 1,077th career assist. He also holds the Big Ten single-season, tournament single-game (16), and career (96) assist records. The most prolific passer in college basketball history.

funFacts.title: Painter's 500th (Purdue)
funFacts.body: The win over Miami was Matt Painter's 500th victory at Purdue. He's been the head coach for 21 years and played at Purdue from 1989-93. Five-time Big Ten Coach of the Year. Won the 2025 Wooden "Legends of Coaching" Award.

funFacts.title: Lithuanian Basketball Royalty (Texas)
funFacts.body: Matas Vokietaitis's mother played for Lithuania's national age-group basketball teams. Basketball is essentially the national religion in Lithuania. Vokietaitis has represented Lithuania at three FIBA international tournaments. He's averaging 18.3 PPG and 11.0 RPG in this tournament.

funFacts.title: Miller's Sweet 16 Magic (Texas)
funFacts.body: Sean Miller is 9-1 all-time in NCAA Tournament games with a Sweet 16 spot on the line — the best winning percentage of any coach with 10+ games since 1975. Better than Krzyzewski (26-6) and Bob Knight (14-4). He's now in his eighth Sweet 16 across Xavier, Arizona, and Texas.

funFacts.title: Purdue's Double-Digit Seed Trauma
funFacts.body: Purdue lost to 16-seed Fairleigh Dickinson (2023), 15-seed Saint Peter's (2022), and 13-seed North Texas (2021). Three straight years of double-digit seed losses. Texas is an 11-seed with house-money energy. The scar tissue is real.

funFacts.title: The Six-Year Man (Texas)
funFacts.body: Tramon Mark is in his sixth year of college basketball across three schools: Houston (2021 Final Four), Arkansas, and Texas. He has four career game-winners under 5 seconds — including the fadeaway that beat NC State in the First Four. He may have more tournament experience than some assistant coaches.

funFacts.title: Cradle of Astronauts (Purdue)
funFacts.body: Purdue has produced 24 astronauts, including Neil Armstrong (first person on the Moon) and Gene Cernan (last person on the Moon). Other notable Purdue alumni: Drew Brees and Orville Redenbacher.

funFacts.title: Loyer's Heater (Purdue)
funFacts.body: Fletcher Loyer scored 24 points on 6-of-7 shooting against Miami and is shooting 54% from three over his last five games. His father is an NBA scout for the Clippers, his grandfather played at Purdue in 1964, and he won the national High School 3-Point Championship.

funFacts.title: 5 Turnovers vs. the Best Defense in America (Texas)
funFacts.body: Texas committed just 5 turnovers against Gonzaga — the team with the No. 1 defense in the country — and had 20 assists on 31 baskets. That's elite ball movement by any standard. For comparison, Braden Smith alone had 8 turnovers against Miami.

funFacts.title: Zero National Championships (Both)
funFacts.body: Neither program has won a men's basketball national championship. Purdue has been to the title game twice (1969, 2024) and lost both times. Texas has 39 tournament appearances — among the most ever — without a title. Somebody's drought continues Thursday.

history.callout: Purdue leads the all-time series 3-2, and the programs have a recent tournament history: Purdue beat Texas 81-71 in the 2022 NCAA Tournament second round, powered by Jaden Ivey's 18 points and a devastating 20-0 first-half run. Now Texas seeks revenge in San Jose — as a bigger underdog but with significantly more tournament momentum.

timeline.text: No. 3 Purdue beat No. 6 Texas 81-71 in the second round of the NCAA Tournament. Jaden Ivey scored 18 and Trevion Williams had 22 off the bench.
Purdue went on a 20-0 run in the first half as Texas went scoreless for nearly 10 minutes. The Longhorns never recovered, despite Marcus Carr's 23 points.

Purdue advanced to the Sweet 16, then lost to 15-seed Saint Peter's in one of the biggest upsets in tournament history. That Purdue team had Ivey, Edey, and Kaufman-Renn — two of those three are in the NBA now.

subsections.title: Texas's Improbable Run to San Jose

timeline.text: Texas 68, NC State 66. Tramon Mark's fadeaway with 1.1 seconds left. A game-winner for the ages. Texas was a team that lost 5 of its last 6 and nearly didn't make the tournament.
Texas 79, BYU 71. Vokietaitis explodes for 21 and 14. AJ Dybantsa scores 35 in what was likely his last college game, but Texas's balanced attack is too much.
Texas 74, Gonzaga 68. Pope and Vokietaitis each score 17. Camden Heide hits the game-sealing corner three with 14.7 seconds left. Three games in five days. The Cinderella run is real.

subsections.title: Purdue's Championship Chase Continues
subsections.content:
Purdue has never won a national championship in men's basketball. They've been to the title game twice — losing to UCLA in 1969 and to UConn in 2024 with Zach Edey. Under Matt Painter (21 years, 500 wins), this program has become a perennial contender: 11 straight tournament appearances, three consecutive Sweet 16s, and the 2024 title game.

But Purdue also knows heartbreak. The 2023 FDU upset (63-58 as a 1-seed) was the second 16-over-1 loss ever. The 2022 loss to Saint Peter's. The 2021 loss to North Texas. Three straight years of double-digit seed upsets before the 2024 redemption run. Now they face another lower-seeded underdog — an 11-seed with momentum, a coach who's 9-1 in these situations, and a former Purdue player on the roster. The championship chase continues, but the ghosts linger.
